metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Replace the 'operator-provided' placeholder with a stated default staging target (e.g. 'use `<org>/rebar-staging` under the same GitHub org, publishing to TestPyPI project `nava-rebar` — confirmed by operator before story start') so the executor can act without a round-trip.
findings.finding: The staging org/fork identity is deferred with 'operator-provided', which is an explicit placeholder for a real configuration decision. An executor performing the operator-attested staging run (AC6) cannot proceed without knowing the target org, fork name, and TestPyPI project — even though the codebase work is unblocked, the attestation workflow (and any staging release.yml setup) cannot be completed.
findings.location: What section — 'Testing safely + the dry-run affordance moves' bullet; AC6

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Update `tests/unit/test_mirror_lock_terraform.py`'s module docstring (line 7) and test comment (line 42) to replace the stale rationale ('tags MUST stay human-pushable so release.yml can publish on a v* tag push') with the new invariant ('tags stay human-pushable as outputs only; publication now requires dispatch-from-main + reviewer-gated pypi environment') — add this to the Scope section's description of what the test-file edit must do.
findings.finding: The plan asserts test_mirror_lock_terraform.py's test_module_is_main_only_no_tag_lock 'codifies no tag lock' and that the test and its no-tag-lock decision are 'unchanged', but the test file's module docstring (line 7) and test comment (line 42) give the REASON as 'tags MUST stay human-pushable so .github/workflows/release.yml can publish to PyPI on a v* tag push' — a premise this plan explicitly revokes. The Scope section lists the test file as needing edits (for the new pypi environment assertion) but does not specify updating the now-false rationale comment, leaving a contradiction baked into the contract test.
findings.location: Scope section / tests/unit/test_mirror_lock_terraform.py lines 6-8, 42
findings.suggested_fix: In the Scope bullet for `tests/unit/test_mirror_lock_terraform.py`, extend the description: 'also update the module docstring and the inline comment in `test_module_is_main_only_no_tag_lock` to replace the stale rationale ("tags MUST stay human-pushable so release.yml can publish on a v* tag push") with the new invariant ("tags stay open; the v* tag is now an output created after publish, not a trigger; publication safety comes from dispatch-from-main + reviewer-gated pypi environment").'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] In the `terraform-drift.yml` scope, explicitly specify a **second job** (e.g. `plan-github`) with `working-directory: infra/terraform-github`, `GITHUB_TOKEN` auth (no AWS credential step), and its own `paths:` trigger for `infra/terraform-github/**` — the existing AWS-OIDC `plan` job cannot authenticate the `integrations/github` provider.
findings.finding: The plan says to add `infra/terraform-github/**` to `terraform-drift.yml`'s `paths:` filters and 'run `terraform init && terraform plan` there', but the existing `plan` job authenticates exclusively via AWS OIDC (`aws-actions/configure-aws-credentials` → IAM role `rebar-terraform-plan`) and sets `working-directory: infra/terraform`. The `infra/terraform-github/` module uses the `integrations/github` provider, which requires a `GITHUB_TOKEN` (not AWS IAM). Simply patching the path globs on the existing job cannot work — a separate job with `GITHUB_TOKEN` and `working-directory: infra/terraform-github` is required. This unenumerated structural requirement means the AC5 implementation target is incomplete as described.
findings.location: Scope section (`terraform-drift.yml`) and AC5
findings.suggested_fix: Add a second job to `terraform-drift.yml` (e.g. `plan-github`) that: (1) does NOT run `aws-actions/configure-aws-credentials`, (2) sets `working-directory: infra/terraform-github`, (3) reads `GITHUB_TOKEN` from secrets (or uses the built-in `github.token` via `env: GITHUB_TOKEN: ${{ github.token }}`), (4) has its own `paths: ['infra/terraform-github/**']` in the `on:` trigger alongside the existing `infra/terraform/**` filter. The existing `plan` job remains unchanged.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify how the `terraform-drift.yml` extension authenticates to the `integrations/github` provider (e.g., a `GITHUB_TOKEN` org/repo secret with `Administration:read` + `environments:read`, or a separate drift job step) — the existing AWS OIDC auth in the drift workflow does not cover the github provider used by `infra/terraform-github/`.
findings.finding: The plan's `terraform-drift.yml` extension is mechanically broken: the existing drift job authenticates via AWS OIDC (configure-aws-credentials → rebar-terraform-plan IAM role) and targets the AWS-backend `infra/terraform/` module. The new `infra/terraform-github/` module uses the `integrations/github` provider, which requires a `GITHUB_TOKEN` with `Administration:write` scope — not AWS credentials. Simply adding `infra/terraform-github/**` to the `paths:` filter and running `terraform plan` in a second working-directory will fail at `terraform init` (github provider auth error) unless a separate GitHub token is provisioned and injected. The plan says 'run `terraform init && terraform plan` there' but does not specify how to authenticate the github provider in the CI context.
findings.location: Scope section — `.github/workflows/terraform-drift.yml` bullet; AC5
findings.suggested_fix: Add a separate job (e.g., `plan-github`) in `terraform-drift.yml` that: (1) injects `GITHUB_TOKEN` from a CI secret with read-only org scope, (2) sets `working-directory: infra/terraform-github`, (3) runs `terraform init -input=false` and `terraform plan -input=false -lock=false -detailed-exitcode`. The existing `plan` job covers the AWS stack unchanged. Update AC5 to name this separate job explicitly.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Explicitly resolve the conflict with the parent epic SC that requires 'a retained `v*` tag-push fallback': either (a) note in this plan that the story intentionally supersedes the fallback requirement (and propose an epic SC amendment) or (b) add a scoped tag-push path that asserts ancestry but cannot reach the `pypi` environment.
findings.finding: The plan directly contradicts the parent epic's Success Criteria, which explicitly require 'a retained `v*` tag-push fallback [that] additionally asserts ancestry to `origin/main`.' The plan instead eliminates the tag-push publish path entirely ('No raw-tag publish path', 'a hand-pushed `v*` tag cannot reach the `pypi` environment and therefore cannot publish'). The plan does not acknowledge this divergence from the parent SC, leaving an unresolved conflict between the story and the epic that will surface at epic closure (epic AC: 'all five child stories are closed').
findings.location: What section — 'No raw-tag publish path' bullet; parent epic 2610-9557-c3c1-460c Success Criteria bullet 1

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify and document the exact Terraform GitHub provider resource schema needed for a custom-branch `deployment_branch_policy` on a GitHub environment (confirm whether `github_repository_environment_deployment_policy` is required alongside `github_repository_environment`), and update the Scope/AC4 HCL description to name both resources if needed.
findings.finding: The plan states the `pypi` environment protection will be codified in Terraform as 'a `github_repository_environment` with a `reviewers` block + a `main`-only `deployment_branch_policy`' — presenting this as a single-resource block. However, the Terraform GitHub provider's custom-branch-pattern policy for environments requires a *second* resource (`github_repository_environment_deployment_policy`) alongside `github_repository_environment` when `custom_branch_policies = true`; the branch pattern itself cannot be expressed as a sub-block of `github_repository_environment`. The plan does not acknowledge this two-resource requirement, leaving the IaC feasibility of the 'single most security-critical gate' partially unverified.
findings.location: What section — 'Human authorization — re-key the `pypi` environment, CODIFIED IN IaC'; Scope section — 'infra/terraform-github/ — codify the `pypi` environment protection: a `github_repository_environment "pypi"` with a `reviewers` block … and a `main`-only `deployment_branch_policy`'; AC4
findings.suggested_fix: In the Scope section, replace the single-resource description with the two-resource pattern if confirmed: 'a `github_repository_environment "pypi"` resource (with `deployment_branch_policy { protected_branches = false; custom_branch_policies = true }` and a `reviewers` block) PLUS a companion `github_repository_environment_deployment_policy "pypi_main"` resource (with `branch_pattern = "main"`)'. Update AC4's contract-test assertion to check for both resources.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Co-locate the HCL implementation protocol for `main`-only deployment-branch policy alongside the AC4 contract-test assertion: specify whether to use `protected_branches = false` + `custom_branch_policies = true` + a companion `github_repository_environment_deployment_policy` resource, or the `protected_branches = true` shorthand, so the contract test and the IaC pattern are aligned.
findings.finding: The Terraform `github_repository_environment "pypi"` `main`-only `deployment_branch_policy` implementation protocol is not co-located with the AC contract-test schema assertion. In the GitHub Terraform provider (>= 6.x), a custom-branch deployment policy requires both a `deployment_branch_policy { protected_branches = false; custom_branch_policies = true }` block inside `github_repository_environment` AND a separate `github_repository_environment_deployment_policy` resource. The plan names only the `github_repository_environment` resource and asserts `deployment_branch_policy` restricted to `main` via a contract test — but which HCL pattern produces that assertion is unspecified, so an implementer using the wrong sub-block structure will pass `terraform validate` but fail the contract test.
findings.location: What section — Human authorization bullet; Scope — infra/terraform-github/; AC4 (github_repository_environment 'pypi')

findings.finding: The plan states 'dry runs relocate to a staging repo / TestPyPI target' but names neither the staging repository nor the TestPyPI project; AC5 references 'staging run URLs on the ticket' but no step tells the executor how to set up or identify the staging target, making the dry-run AC operationally ambiguous for the person who must run it.
findings.location: What section — 'Testing safely + the dry-run affordance moves'; AC5 (operator-attested)
findings.suggested_fix: Add a sentence: 'Staging target: the TestPyPI project `<package-name>-staging` (pre-configured with OIDC trusted publishing from the `<org>/<repo>-staging` fork), exercised via a dispatch on that fork's `release.yml`.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Resolve the tag-fallback conflict: either (a) update the parent epic's Success Criteria to drop "a retained `v*` tag-push fallback additionally asserts ancestry to `origin/main`" in favour of the leaf's one-path model before re-reviewing this leaf, or (b) reintroduce a tag-push path with ancestry assertion into the leaf to match the parent's two-path design.
findings.finding: The leaf eliminates the v* tag-push publish path entirely, but the parent's Success Criteria explicitly requires retaining it as a fallback with an ancestry assertion: "a retained `v*` tag-push fallback additionally asserts ancestry to `origin/main`." The leaf redefines this deliverable differently — one-path security (dispatch-only, tag dead-ended at environment) vs. the parent's two-path design (dispatch primary + tag fallback with ancestry guard). Per the G7 conflict rule, the parent wins.
findings.location: Parent SC (epic 2610-9557-c3c1-460c, first bullet of Success Criteria) vs. leaf What § "No raw-tag publish path", leaf Scope ("Remove the push: tags: v* publish trigger"), leaf AC #1 ("no push: tags publish trigger")
findings.suggested_fix: Update the parent epic (2610-9557-c3c1-460c) Success Criteria first bullet to remove the "retained v* tag-push fallback" clause (replacing it with "a hand-pushed v* tag cannot reach the pypi environment by construction") before re-reviewing this leaf. This makes the parent consistent with the leaf's one-path design and triggers the required parent plan-review re-attestation.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify that expressing a `main`-only custom branch pattern in the Terraform GitHub provider v6.12.1 requires a companion `github_repository_environment_deployment_policy` resource (separate from the `github_repository_environment` block), and if so: (a) add that resource to the Scope's IaC changes, and (b) extend the AC4 contract test to assert the companion resource's branch pattern is present in `main.tf`.
findings.finding: The plan describes the `pypi` environment's `main`-only deployment-branch policy as a sub-block of a single `github_repository_environment` resource, but the Terraform GitHub provider (v6.12.1, locked in .terraform.lock.hcl) requires custom branch patterns to be expressed via a *separate* companion resource `github_repository_environment_deployment_policy` — the sub-block `deployment_branch_policy { custom_branch_policies = true }` alone only *enables* the custom-policy mode but defines no patterns, leaving the environment unrestricted until the companion resource is also applied. The plan's AC4 contract test checks for `deployment_branch_policy` inside the environment block, so it will pass even if the companion resource (and therefore the actual branch restriction) is absent.
findings.location: What section — 'Human authorization — re-key the `pypi` environment, CODIFIED IN IaC'; Scope section — 'infra/terraform-github/ — codify the `pypi` environment protection'; AC4
findings.suggested_fix: In `infra/terraform-github/main.tf`, add both resources: `github_repository_environment "pypi"` with `deployment_branch_policy { protected_branches = false; custom_branch_policies = true }` AND a separate `github_repository_environment_deployment_policy "pypi_main"` resource with `environment = github_repository_environment.pypi.environment` and `branch_pattern = "main"`. Update the AC4 contract test to assert both the `github_repository_environment_deployment_policy` resource and its `branch_pattern = "main"` are present in the HCL.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify how `GITHUB_TOKEN` (with at minimum `read:org` + repo environment-read scope) is supplied to the extended terraform-drift job for `infra/terraform-github/` — e.g., a read-only GitHub App token via `secrets.GITHUB_TOKEN` scoped to the drift workflow, or a dedicated read-scoped PAT stored as a repo/org secret — so `terraform plan` on the GitHub module can authenticate and actually detect drift.
findings.finding: The plan extends `terraform-drift.yml` to run `terraform plan` against `infra/terraform-github/`, but that module uses the GitHub Terraform provider which requires a `GITHUB_TOKEN`. The existing drift workflow authenticates exclusively via AWS OIDC → `rebar-terraform-plan` (an AWS IAM role). There is no provision in the plan for how the extended drift job obtains a GitHub token, so `terraform init`/`plan` on the GitHub module will fail provider initialization — the stated drift-detection goal is not achievable as written.
findings.location: Scope: `.github/workflows/terraform-drift.yml` extension; AC5
findings.suggested_fix: Add a separate job (or step group) in `terraform-drift.yml` for the GitHub module: supply `GITHUB_TOKEN` via `env: GITHUB_TOKEN: ${{ secrets.GITHUB_READONLY_TOKEN }}` (a PAT with `read:org` + repo scope stored as a repo secret), with `working-directory: infra/terraform-github`. This is separate from the AWS OIDC flow; the two providers authenticate independently.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a Scope entry (and optionally an AC step or docs/releasing.md instruction) for configuring the `<org>/rebar-staging` fork: its `release.yml`, TestPyPI OIDC trusted-publishing registration, and the `nava-rebar` TestPyPI project — so the negative-path testing described in 'What' has a concrete, assigned deliverable.
findings.finding: The 'What' section describes a required staging/TestPyPI fork (`<org>/rebar-staging` fork with its own `release.yml` publishing to TestPyPI `nava-rebar`) as necessary for negative testing, but this fork's setup and configuration are not listed as a Scope deliverable, and AC6 (operator-attested) only requires 'staging run URLs' without specifying that the fork must be set up. An implementer has no codebase deliverable to produce for the staging fork.
findings.location: What section ('dry runs relocate to a named staging target: a <org>/rebar-staging fork') vs. Scope section (no entry for staging fork) vs. AC6

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Declare the credential and its access posture for the `terraform-drift.yml` GitHub-provider job: specify whether it uses a fine-grained PAT (minimum `administration:read` on `navapbc/rebar`), a GitHub App OIDC token, or another mechanism; document its permission scope, storage location (Actions secret name), and rotation policy in the plan or in `infra/terraform-github/` comments.
findings.finding: The plan extends `terraform-drift.yml` to run `terraform plan` on `infra/terraform-github/`, but the GitHub Terraform provider requires a GitHub token (distinct from the AWS OIDC credentials the current workflow already obtains). The plan never declares what credential is used, its required permission scope, how it is stored (Actions secret? OIDC?), or its rotation policy — leaving the access posture of this new boundary-crossing path entirely undeclared.
findings.location: Scope — `.github/workflows/terraform-drift.yml` bullet; AC line 5
findings.suggested_fix: Prefer a short-lived credential: configure a GitHub App (or GitHub's OIDC-to-fine-grained-token pattern if available) with `administration:read` scoped only to `navapbc/rebar` for plan-only read operations. If a PAT must be used, store it as an Actions repo secret `GITHUB_TOKEN_TF_PLAN`, declare `administration: read` as its minimum scope, and add a rotation reminder to the runbook. State this explicitly in the Scope bullet and the AC.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a docs/releasing.md subsection (or a dedicated docs/staging-setup.md) covering: how to bootstrap the `rebar-staging` fork (fork setup, TestPyPI OIDC trusted-publishing registration, required GitHub environment config), how to keep its `release.yml` in sync with production, and who owns it — and reference this from the operator-attested AC item so reviewers can verify completeness.
findings.finding: The staging fork (`rebar-staging`) is introduced as the new home for dry-run / negative-path testing but has no documentation plan and its ongoing cross-repo coupling to production `release.yml` is unacknowledged and unmitigated.
findings.location: What section — 'Testing safely + the dry-run affordance moves' bullet; Scope section (Out of scope note); AC item 6 (operator-attested staging)
findings.suggested_fix: Add a 'Staging environment' subsection to docs/releasing.md that specifies: (1) fork the repo to `<org>/rebar-staging`, (2) register `nava-rebar` on test.pypi.org and configure OIDC trusted publishing pointing at the fork's release.yml, (3) add a `pypi` environment in the fork with the same reviewer/branch policy, (4) after any production release.yml change, apply the same structural change to the fork's release.yml before the next staging validation run. Also add a one-line note in the AC: 'staging fork setup documented in docs/releasing.md#staging-environment'.

findings.finding: The 'What' section contains a behavioral hunk — 'Record the approved SHA, version, manifest versions, and artifact hashes in release evidence' — that has no corresponding file target in the Scope section and no matching Acceptance Criterion, leaving the implementation target ambiguous (workflow step? separate artifact? docs section? a new file?).
findings.location: What section (last bullet); Scope section; Acceptance Criteria

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the `version_input` format contract (bare PEP 440 semver, no `v` prefix, matching `server.json` and `pyproject.toml`) and specify whether the lockstep step normalizes before comparison or rejects non-conforming input.
findings.finding: The `version_input` workflow_dispatch input's format contract is never defined: the plan's lockstep equality rule (`version_input == project.version == server.json.version == each server.json.packages[].version`) depends on string equality, but `server.json` stores bare semver (`"0.7.1"`) while the tag-derivation pattern used throughout the existing workflow strips a `v` prefix (`TAG#v`). Whether `version_input` must be bare (`1.2.3`) or `v`-prefixed (`v1.2.3`) — and whether the step normalizes before comparing — is unspecified, leaving the comparison protocol ambiguous for the implementer.
findings.location: What section — Version-lockstep bullet; AC1 (version-lockstep step); existing release.yml:141,160 (TAG#v pattern)
findings.suggested_fix: Add to AC1: 'The version input is validated as a bare PEP 440 version (e.g. `1.2.3`, no `v` prefix); the lockstep step compares it directly (without stripping) against the bare version strings in `pyproject.toml` and `server.json`. Input validation rejects `v`-prefixed values before the lockstep check runs.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Strengthen the AC4 contract-test description to assert a minimum reviewer count (e.g. `len(reviewers) >= 1`) and document whether a self-reviewer is disallowed, so the test proves the second-actor gate rather than merely 'the list is non-empty'.
findings.finding: AC4's contract-test assertion is specified as 'non-empty `reviewers` list' — a boolean check that passes with exactly 1 entry. This is a verify-command cardinality defect (VERIFY-COMMAND-DEFECT type 5): the criterion names a security property (second-human approval) whose meaningful minimum is ≥ 1 *non-committer* reviewer, but the stated assertion would pass even with a self-approving single-entry list.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC4
findings.suggested_fix: Change AC4's test description to: 'asserts the block contains `len(reviewers) >= 1` (or ≥ N per policy) and that the `deployment_branch_policy` is restricted to `main`.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the release evidence lifecycle in the plan: name the storage location (e.g., GitHub Release asset, workflow job summary, or a signed SLSA attestation bundle), the retention policy, who consumes it for audit, and whether/how old evidence records are cleaned up — so the T9 CREATE/CONSUME/RETIRE lifecycle is complete.
findings.finding: Release evidence storage lifecycle is unspecified: the plan mentions 'Record the approved SHA, version, manifest versions, and artifact hashes in release evidence' but does not specify where this evidence is stored (workflow summary, a release asset, a repo file, an external store), who creates it, how long it is retained, who consumes it for audit, and whether it accumulates unboundedly or has a cleanup path.
findings.location: What section — last bullet of 'What': 'Record the approved SHA, version, manifest versions, and artifact hashes in release evidence'

findings.finding: The plan names the `pypi` GitHub environment's required-reviewers gate as 'the single most security-critical gate' but never declares (a) whether the workflow dispatcher can self-approve the environment review, or (b) the minimum reviewer count or which team/users are eligible reviewers. GitHub's environment protection allows self-approval by default unless explicitly disabled; if a single actor with `actions:write` can both dispatch and self-approve, the reviewer gate is hollow and the plan's central authorization control is bypassed.
findings.location: What section — 'Human authorization — re-key the `pypi` environment' bullet; AC line 'The `pypi` environment protection (required reviewers + `main`-only deployment-branch policy) is codified in `infra/terraform-github/`'
findings.suggested_fix: In the Terraform `github_repository_environment` resource for `pypi`, add: `prevent_self_review = true` and specify at least one named team (e.g., a `maintainers` or `release-approvers` team) in the `reviewers` block. Document in `docs/releasing.md` that the dispatcher cannot self-approve and which team holds approval authority. Add this as a codebase-verifiable AC: 'the `pypi` environment Terraform resource sets `prevent_self_review = true` and names a specific reviewer team.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit rollback / back-out plan (e.g., an expand-contract sequence: first add the dispatch trigger alongside the existing push-tags trigger and verify end-to-end, then remove the push-tags trigger only after a successful dispatch-based publish) or document a time-bounded emergency fallback procedure in `docs/releasing.md`.
findings.finding: The plan removes the push-tags publish trigger and re-keys the `pypi` environment from 'v* tags only' to 'main via workflow_dispatch' — both destructive/irreversible operational changes — but provides no rollback path or expand-contract sequencing. If the new dispatch mechanism is misconfigured (e.g., the Terraform apply has not yet run, the environment reviewers block is wrong, or the OIDC trusted-publishing registration is stale), there is no described way to revert to the old tag-push path to unblock an urgent release.
findings.location: What section ('Build, then publish DIRECTLY via PyPA Trusted Publishing — no tag relay', 'No raw-tag publish path') and Scope ('infra/terraform-github/ — codify the pypi environment protection')
findings.suggested_fix: Adopt an expand-contract sequence: (1) Add `workflow_dispatch` trigger and the re-keyed environment in a first PR, leaving `push: tags` in place but inert (environment now only accepts dispatch); (2) perform a full dispatch-based release to TestPyPI and then production; (3) in a follow-up PR remove the now-dead `push: tags` trigger. This gives a clear back-out: if step 2 fails, revert the environment policy and the old trigger still works.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC for the release-evidence deliverable: specify the content schema (approved SHA, version string, manifest versions, artifact hashes), the job that writes it (e.g. a post-publish step), the format (GitHub job summary and/or uploaded artifact), and whether evidence-write failure should block the run.
findings.finding: The 'release evidence' deliverable (last What bullet: 'Record the approved SHA, version, manifest versions, and artifact hashes in release evidence') has no corresponding acceptance criterion, no content schema (field names, format), no specified job placement in the workflow, and no fallback for evidence-write failure. This is a structural gap: a processing output with undefined vocabulary and no verifiability.
findings.location: What section — last bullet ('Record the approved SHA…'); Acceptance Criteria (AC1–AC9 — none covers this deliverable)

findings.finding: Acceptance-criteria / definition-of-done quality issues.
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State the minimum required-reviewer count (e.g. ≥ 1 named non-author, or ≥ 2) in the `infra/terraform-github/` scope bullet and update AC4's contract-test assertion to check `len(reviewers) >= N` rather than merely `non-empty`.
findings.finding: The required-reviewer count for the `pypi` GitHub environment is never stated. The plan says 'required reviewers' and 'non-empty reviewers' throughout, but the terraform `github_repository_environment` `reviewers` block requires a concrete list of users/teams. An executor cannot write the IaC or the contract test's cardinality check without choosing a number — yet the plan provides no default.
findings.location: Scope / `infra/terraform-github/` bullet; AC4 (`test_pypi_environment_has_required_reviewers_and_main_only_policy`)
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the scope bullet: 'The `reviewers` block must list at least one named reviewer (GitHub user ID or team slug) distinct from the committer — codify this minimum in the contract test as `assert len(reviewers) >= 1 and reviewer_ids != {committer_id}`.' Adjust to ≥ 2 if org policy requires two-person rule.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add to `docs/releasing.md` (and reference in AC) the non-happy-path exits for the reviewer gate: (a) what to do when a reviewer rejects the deployment, (b) what to do when the GitHub environment approval timer expires, and (c) whether a cancelled/rejected run is safe to re-dispatch (no partial PyPI state to clean up, since OIDC publish is atomic).
findings.finding: The plan introduces a new human-in-the-loop reviewer approval gate (the `pypi` GitHub environment with required reviewers), but neither the Acceptance Criteria nor the `docs/releasing.md` scope covers the non-happy-path exit points of that gate: what a release engineer should do when a reviewer explicitly rejects the deployment, or when the GitHub environment approval timer expires before approval is granted (the workflow is left in a dangling 'waiting' state, then cancelled).
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — operator-attested items; 'docs/releasing.md' scope paragraph; 'Human authorization' section of What
findings.suggested_fix: In the `docs/releasing.md` section, add a 'Recovery procedures' sub-section covering: (1) Reviewer rejects → fix the root cause, re-dispatch from main (no cleanup needed — publish never ran); (2) Approval timer expires → re-dispatch (same); (3) Partial-failure after publish but before tag creation → manually create the tag and GitHub Release pointing at the known-good SHA, since PyPI upload is not rolled back. Also add an AC line: 'docs/releasing.md documents reviewer-rejection and approval-timeout recovery steps.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a recovery/resumption path for partial-publish state: document (in `docs/releasing.md` and the workflow) what to do when PyPI publish succeeds but a later step (tag creation, GitHub Release, MCP registry) fails — e.g., a separate `workflow_dispatch` input to skip the publish step and resume from tag creation, or idempotency guards (check-tag-exists before create, check MCP entry before register).
findings.finding: Partial-publish stuck state: if the PyPI publish step succeeds but the subsequent tag-creation or GitHub Release step fails, a re-run will fail at the PyPI publish step with a duplicate-version rejection (PyPI rejects re-upload of an existing version). The plan specifies 'tag is an output, not a trigger' but gives no recovery path for this stuck state, leaving the release with a published PyPI package but no tag or GitHub Release and no documented way to resume.
findings.location: What section — 'Tag + GitHub Release created AFTER a successful publish'; AC line 3 ('creates the v* tag + GitHub Release only after a successful publish')
findings.suggested_fix: In `release.yml`, guard the tag-creation step with `git tag --list v$VERSION | grep -q .` and skip-if-exists; guard the GitHub Release creation with the `gh release view` check; guard MCP registration similarly. Document these guards in `docs/releasing.md` as the recovery procedure. This makes each post-publish step idempotent so a re-run safely resumes from where it failed.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a `concurrency:` group to `release.yml` (e.g., `concurrency: { group: release-${{ inputs.version }}, cancel-in-progress: false }`) so only one release workflow runs at a time per version, preventing concurrent-dispatch races on the shared PyPI version slot and git tag namespace.
findings.finding: Concurrent-dispatch race on shared git tag and PyPI version: the plan places no mutual-exclusion guard on simultaneous `workflow_dispatch` runs. Two operators could both dispatch the same version at the same time; both would pass the ref guard, version-lockstep, and ancestry checks (all read-only at that stage), then race at the PyPI publish step (second fails with 400) and the git tag creation step (second fails). The plan does not address this concurrency scenario or the resulting partial-state cleanup.
findings.location: What section — 'Human authorization — re-key the pypi environment'; Scope — '.github/workflows/release.yml'
findings.suggested_fix:
Add to `release.yml` at the workflow level:
```yaml
concurrency:
  group: release-${{ github.workflow }}-${{ inputs.version }}
  cancel-in-progress: false
```
This queues rather than cancels, ensuring a second approved run waits for the first to complete (and then fails cleanly at the PyPI duplicate-version check with a clear error).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm whether the staging fork + TestPyPI OIDC registration is justified over a simpler alternative (e.g., a dedicated `release-dry-run.yml` workflow that runs the lockstep + ancestry checks against main without publishing, or a TestPyPI target within the same repo's release.yml triggered by a separate dispatch input flag), given zero prior infrastructure exists and the removed in-repo dry-run already served this purpose.
findings.finding: The staging fork (`<org>/rebar-staging`) + TestPyPI project registration is new external infrastructure with zero prior codebase presence, introduced solely to replace the simpler in-repo `workflow_dispatch` dry-run affordance that the plan explicitly removes. For a small single-maintainer project with infrequent releases, this adds cross-repo setup, a separate TestPyPI OIDC trusted-publisher registration, and an ongoing maintenance surface that serves the same negative-test purpose as the removed in-repo dry-run — more complex infrastructure for no additional security or reliability gain.
findings.location: What section — 'Testing safely + the dry-run affordance moves' bullet; AC6 (operator-attested staging run URLs)
findings.suggested_fix: Replace the cross-repo staging fork with a `dry_run` boolean input on the same `workflow_dispatch`: when `true`, run all pre-publish checks (ref guard, version lockstep, ancestry, build) but substitute `repository-url: https://test.pypi.org/legacy/` in the publish step (or skip publish). This keeps negative-path testing in-repo with no new external infrastructure, mirrors the pattern the plan's own 'removed dry-run' was already serving.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] In the `tests/unit/test_mirror_lock_terraform.py` scope edit, also update the stale inline comment at line 42 (`# tags stay human-pushable (release.yml v* pushes) -> NO tag lock ruleset`) to reflect the new rationale: tags stay human-pushable because the workflow creates them as post-publish outputs, not because the workflow is triggered by them.
findings.finding: The plan enumerates `tests/unit/test_mirror_lock_terraform.py` as a scope target (to add the new `test_pypi_environment_has_required_reviewers_and_main_only_policy` assertion), but line 42 of that file contains the comment `# tags stay human-pushable (release.yml v* pushes) -> NO tag lock ruleset`. After this plan removes the `push: tags: v*` trigger from `release.yml` and makes tags an output (not a trigger), this inline comment becomes stale and misleading — it documents the old rationale for the 'no tag lock' decision, which was specifically 'release.yml needs to push v* tags to publish'. The new rationale is different (tags are output-only; publication safety comes from the environment gate). The plan does not explicitly call out updating this comment.
findings.location: tests/unit/test_mirror_lock_terraform.py line 42; Scope section (test_mirror_lock_terraform.py)
findings.suggested_fix:
Change line 42 comment to: `# tags stay human-pushable: the release workflow creates v* tags AFTER publish (output, not trigger)
    # — see .github/workflows/release.yml dispatch trigger`

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an idempotency guard to the publish job (e.g., check whether the version already exists on PyPI before invoking `pypa/gh-action-pypi-publish`, and skip with a success exit if it does) so that a re-dispatch after a partial publish completes the remaining post-publish jobs instead of failing on the PyPI step.
findings.finding: The plan does not address the partial-publish stuck state: if the PyPI `publish` job succeeds but the subsequent `v*` tag creation or GitHub Release job fails, re-dispatching the workflow will pass the version-lockstep check (pyproject.toml is still bumped) but then fail the PyPI publish step with 'File already exists on PyPI' — leaving the release permanently stuck with a PyPI artifact but no git tag or GitHub Release. This is a TOCTOU-style ordering assumption: the plan describes the tag as 'record only' but does not specify how an operator recovers when the record-creation step fails after the irreversible publish.
findings.location: What section — 'Tag + GitHub Release created AFTER a successful publish' bullet; AC3
findings.suggested_fix: Before the `pypa/gh-action-pypi-publish` step, add: `if curl -s -o /dev/null -w '%{http_code}' "https://pypi.org/pypi/nava-rebar/$VERSION/json" | grep -qx 200; then echo 'Already published, skipping.'; else <publish step>; fi`. Alternatively, document a recovery runbook in `docs/releasing.md` covering the tag-only / release-only re-run path.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `lifecycle { prevent_destroy = true }` to the new `github_repository_environment "pypi"` resource in `infra/terraform-github/main.tf` and add a corresponding contract-test assertion in `tests/unit/test_mirror_lock_terraform.py` that the `pypi` environment block contains `prevent_destroy = true`.
findings.finding: The plan adds a `github_repository_environment "pypi"` resource described as 'the single most security-critical gate,' but does not specify a `prevent_destroy = true` lifecycle guard on it. Accidental `terraform destroy` or an inadvertent removal of the resource block would silently drop required reviewers from the `pypi` environment, enabling single-actor publication without triggering any alarm.
findings.location: Scope: `infra/terraform-github/` — `github_repository_environment "pypi"` resource; What section: 'Human authorization' bullet
findings.suggested_fix:
In the new `github_repository_environment "pypi"` block, add:
```hcl
lifecycle {
  prevent_destroy = true
}
```
And extend the contract test to assert `re.search(r'prevent_destroy\s*=\s*true', pypi_env_block)`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Update the `test_mirror_lock_terraform.py:42` comment in the Scope/task list to replace the now-false rationale `'(release.yml v* pushes)'` with the correct post-change rationale (e.g. 'tags are outputs, not triggers; publication safety comes from dispatch-from-main + reviewer-gated environment').
findings.finding: Pink-elephant antipattern: `tests/unit/test_mirror_lock_terraform.py:42` contains the inline rationale `# tags stay human-pushable (release.yml v* pushes) -> NO tag lock ruleset`. After this plan lands, `release.yml` no longer publishes on a `v*` tag push — dispatch-from-`main` is the sole publish path. The plan's Scope section instructs adding a new test assertion to this file but says nothing about updating the now-false comment. A future editor reading the test will be told the reason tags must stay pushable is 'release.yml v* pushes', which will be factually wrong, potentially causing them to reason that removing the tag-push trigger from release.yml restores the tag-lock option.
findings.location: tests/unit/test_mirror_lock_terraform.py:42; Scope section — 'tests/unit/test_mirror_lock_terraform.py — add the pypi-environment contract-test assertion'
findings.suggested_fix: In the Scope section, change 'add the pypi-environment contract-test assertion alongside the existing no-tag-lock test' to 'add the pypi-environment contract-test assertion alongside the existing no-tag-lock test AND update the no-tag-lock test comment to reflect the new rationale (tags are record-only outputs; publication safety comes from dispatch-from-main + the reviewer-gated pypi environment, not from the v*-push trigger).'

findings.finding: The `terraform-drift.yml` CI workflow only scans `infra/terraform/**` (its `paths:` filter on lines 14 and 17), not `infra/terraform-github/**`. The plan adds a `github_repository_environment` resource to `infra/terraform-github/main.tf` — the single most security-critical gate per the plan — but this directory is outside the drift-detection workflow's path filter, so applied drift in the `pypi` environment protection will go undetected by CI.
findings.location: .github/workflows/terraform-drift.yml lines 14-17 / infra/terraform-github/ Scope section
findings.suggested_fix: Add `'infra/terraform-github/**'` to both `paths:` arrays in `.github/workflows/terraform-drift.yml` (pull_request trigger and push trigger), and extend the drift job to also run `terraform init && terraform plan` in `infra/terraform-github/` with appropriate credentials.

findings.finding: The existing terraform-drift.yml workflow is scoped to `infra/terraform/**` and uses `working-directory: infra/terraform`; it does NOT cover `infra/terraform-github/**` where the new `github_repository_environment "pypi"` resource will reside. The plan asserts the new resource is 'code-reviewed and drift-detected in IaC,' but that drift-detection claim is hollow for this module unless the workflow is extended.
findings.location: Plan 'What' section ('codified in infra/terraform-github/ … drift-detected'); .github/workflows/terraform-drift.yml lines 13-31
findings.suggested_fix:
Add a second job (or extend the paths filter and add a working-directory matrix entry) to terraform-drift.yml:
```yaml
  plan-github:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    defaults:
      run:
        working-directory: infra/terraform-github
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v7
      - name: Setup Terraform
        uses: hashicorp/setup-terraform@v4
        with:
          terraform_version: 1.10.5
          terraform_wrapper: false
      - name: terraform init
        run: terraform init -input=false
      - name: terraform plan (fail on drift)
        env:
          GITHUB_TOKEN: ${{ secrets.GITHUB_TOKEN }}
        run: terraform plan -input=false -lock=false -detailed-exitcode
```
Also add `infra/terraform-github/**` to the `pull_request` and `push` paths filters.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC criterion (or Scope bullet) explicitly verifying that the old in-repo dry-run affordance is removed from `release.yml` (e.g., no `dry_run`/non-publishing dispatch conditional remains in the workflow).
findings.finding: The 'What' section explicitly states 'that in-repo dry-run affordance is removed' from release.yml, but the Scope section's release.yml entry and no AC criterion verify this removal. The Scope lists 'Remove the push: tags: v* publish trigger' but does not list removing the existing dry-run dispatch path. An implementer could satisfy all AC criteria without removing the old dry-run affordance, leaving an ambiguous dispatch that behaves differently than described.
findings.location: What section ('dry runs relocate to a named staging target') vs. Scope section ('release.yml') vs. AC (no covering criterion)
findings.suggested_fix: Add to AC1: 'and any prior dry-run conditional path (dispatch-as-non-publishing) is absent from the workflow' OR add a Scope bullet under release.yml: 'remove the existing dry-run dispatch affordance (dry runs relocate to the staging fork).'

findings.finding: AC4's stated proving command for the security-critical environment protection — 'the terraform resource existing and, where the repo has a terraform-config contract test, by that test' — relies on an existing contract test (`test_mirror_lock_terraform.py`) that tests the no-tag-lock property, NOT the new `github_repository_environment` resource's `reviewers` or `deployment_branch_policy`. No plan step describes adding a new contract-test assertion for these fields, so the proving command for the most critical AC either silently passes on the old test (which doesn't cover the new resource) or the conditional 'where the repo has a contract test' makes verification ambiguous.
findings.location: AC4; What section — 'Human authorization'; Scope — `infra/terraform-github/`
findings.suggested_fix: Revise AC4 to read: '…verified by the terraform resource existing and by a new contract-test assertion `tests/unit/test_mirror_lock_terraform.py::test_pypi_environment_has_required_reviewers_and_main_only_policy` that parses `main.tf` and asserts the `github_repository_environment "pypi"` block contains a non-empty `reviewers` list and a `deployment_branch_policy` whose `custom_branch_policies = false` or branch-name filter equals `main`.'

findings.finding: The Acceptance Criteria for the new `github_repository_environment` Terraform resource — which the plan identifies as 'the single most security-critical gate' — conditions contract-test coverage on whether a test already exists ('where the repo has a terraform-config contract test, by that test') rather than mandating that a new contract test be added for the new resource's critical properties (reviewers block, main-only deployment_branch_policy). If no existing test covers these new properties, the security-critical environment control ships with no automated regression guard, and a future terraform change silently removing `reviewers` or widening the branch policy would not be caught.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC4 / Scope: infra/terraform-github/
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the Scope section under `infra/terraform-github/`: 'Add or extend a terraform contract test (following `test_mirror_lock_terraform.py`) asserting the `github_repository_environment "pypi"` resource has: (a) at least one entry in `reviewers`, and (b) a `deployment_branch_policy` restricted to `main`.' Update AC4 to: 'Verified by the terraform resource existing AND a contract test asserting the `reviewers` block is non-empty and the `deployment_branch_policy` is `main`-only.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add permalink citations (direct GitHub workflow-file URLs with line-range anchors, or specific release tag) for the uv and pydantic prior-art patterns cited in the Why/What sections, so the attributed designs are verifiable by implementers and reviewers.
findings.finding: The plan names uv and pydantic as the specific prior-art models being adopted ('Adopt uv's proven release process and pydantic's pre-build version check', 'pydantic pattern', '(uv model)') but provides no URLs, workflow-file paths, or release-tag citations for either project's actual implementation. An implementer cannot verify whether uv actually implements a required-reviewers environment in the described shape, or whether pydantic's version check is a standalone pre-build job (rather than inside a build hook or post-build step), making these prior-art anchors unverifiable as written.
findings.location: Why section (opening paragraph 'uv model'); What section — first paragraph 'Adopt uv's proven release process and pydantic's pre-build version check'; 'Version-lockstep, before build (pydantic pattern)' bullet
findings.suggested_fix: In the 'What' opening paragraph, replace the bare names with pinned links, e.g.: 'uv's `workflow_dispatch` + environment gate (https://github.com/astral-sh/uv/blob/<SHA>/.github/workflows/release.yml)' and 'pydantic's pre-build version check (https://github.com/pydantic/pydantic/blob/<SHA>/.github/workflows/ci.yml#L…)'. If the exact files cannot be located, note that the pattern is independently justified by the codebase's own needs and remove the prior-art framing.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a 'Decision rationale' subsection to docs/releasing.md (or a new `docs/adr/release-authorization.md`) capturing: the threat that motivated the change, why dispatch-from-main + reviewer-gated environment is preferred over tag-based authorization, and the key tradeoffs — so the decision is version-controlled and code-reviewed alongside the implementation.
findings.finding: The docs/releasing.md AC specifies documenting the *process and invariants* but not the *decision rationale* (why dispatch-from-main over tag-based authorization, the tradeoffs, the threat model), leaving the novel architectural decision uncaptured in any in-repo artifact.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — last AC item: 'docs/releasing.md documents the dispatch-from-main process, the version-lockstep invariant, the environment re-key, and the reviewer requirement'

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
